coredata.dc:title: A di-functional and label-free carbon-based chem-nanosensor for real-time monitoring of pH fluctuation and quantitative determining of Curcumin
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Xiaojuan
Wang, Huiping
Liu, Yang
Hu, Qin
Gao, Yifang
Yang, Zhenhua
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description:
A nitrogen and sulfur dual-doped carbon nanodots (N,S-CNDs) sample was fabricated by an one-step hydrothermal treatment of acid fuchsin. The obtained N,S-CNDs possess yellow fluorescent, excellent water solubility, high fluorescence stability and religious biocompatibility, which can be used as a di-functional and label-free chem-nanosensor for the determination of pH and curcumin (Cur). When the pH is decreased from 8.0 to 4.4, the N,S-CNDs reveals an extraordinary emission escalation at emission wavelength (λem) of 543 nm, and the pK
a value of the N,S-CNDs was calculated as 6.06. The N,S-CNDs displays a favourable linear relationship in the physiological pH range of 5.0–7.4, which is meaningful for the research of near-neutral cytosolic pH. Furthermore, the laser scanning confocal microscopic images of intracellular distribution and the determination of pH in HeLa cells were successfully carried out, implying that the N,S-CNDs possess excellent cell membrane permeability and are capable of being further applied to real-time pH fluctuations monitoring in live cells with negligible autofluorescence. More importantly, the fluorescence of N,S-CNDs may be dramatically quenched by Cur via a combination of electrostatic and hydrogen-bond interaction and fluorescence inner filter effect (FIFE). The limit of detection is as low as 81 nmol/L Cur, and the linearity range is 0.5–20 μmol/L Cur. Ultimately, a satisfactory result was obtained when applying the as-constructed fluorescent chem-nanosensing system for the analyses of Cur in practical samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Yellow fluorescent N,S-CNDs
Real-time monitoring of pH
Live cells
Quantitative determining of curcumin
Real samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The fabrication of nitrogen and sulfur dual-doped carbon nanodots (N,S-CNDs)
Fluorescence assay of pH and fluorescence pH titrations
Preparation of samples and calibration curves
Optical properties of the as-fabricated N,S-CNDs
Biocompatibility and application of the N,S-CNDs in real-time pH-monitoring of live cells
Mechanism for the recognition of curcumin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra of acid fuchsin (blank line) and N,S-CNDs (red line). (B) XPS survey scan, (C) C 1s XPS, (D) N 1s XPS, (E) O 1s XPS and (F) S 2p XPS of the as-fabricated N,S-CNDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
TGA curve of acid fuchsin and the N,S-CNDs.
The representative (A) TEM image and (B) particle diameter distribution histogram of the as-synthesized N,S-CNDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption (blank line), fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of the N,S-CNDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg/mL). The inset shows photographs of the N,S-CNDs aqueous solution under visible (left) and UV light (right). (B) Fluorescence behavior of the N,S-CNDs at different λex. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of 0.1 mg/mL N,S-CNDs as pH increases from 4.4 to 8.0 at λex of 472 nm. (B) The sigmoidal curve between the fluorescence intensity at λex/λem of 472/543 nm and pHs. (C) The linear plot of (F
0 - F)/F
0 against pH (5.0–7.4). F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CNDs at pH 5.0 and other pHs, respectively.

LSCM images of 0.50 mg/mL N,S-CNDs in HeLa cells at pH (D) 7.4, (E) 6.0 and (F) 5.0. (A), (B) and (C) are the bright field images at pH 7.4, 6.0 and 5.0, respectively. The (G), (H) and (I) are merged images of (A) and (D), (B) and (E) and (C) and (F) at pH 7.4, 6.0 and 5.0, respectively. (J) Intracellular fluorescence intensities of the N,S-CNDs at pH 7.4, 6.0 and 5.0. Data are expressed as a mean ± standard deviation. The λex/λem are 488/490–600 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N,S-CNDs aqueous solution in the presence of Cur at various concentrations (0.0–80 μmol/L) from top to bottom. (B) The plot of fluorescence intensity of N,S-CNDs against Cur concentration in the range of 0.5–20 μmol/L. (C) The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,S-CNDs and Cur. (D) The overlap between the absorption spectrum of Cur and the excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CNDs. (E) Fluorescence decay curve of the N,S-CNDs (0.1 mg/mL) without and with different concentration of Cur at a λex/λem of 405/543 nm.
Graphic illustration of the fabrication method of nitrogen and sulfur dual-doped carbon nanodots (N,S-CNDs) by hydrothermal treatment of acid fuchsin.
Comparison of the proposed method with CNDs employed for Cur determination.
(A) The determination results of Cur in real samples (n = 6, μmol/L). (B) Determination of Cur in real sample.

head.ce:title: A di-functional and label-free carbon-based chem-nanosensor for real-time monitoring of pH fluctuation and quantitative determining of Curcumin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, 225001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A nitrogen and sulfur dual-doped carbon nanodots (N,S-CNDs) sample was fabricated by an one-step hydrothermal treatment of acid fuchsin. The obtained N,S-CNDs possess yellow fluorescent, excellent water solubility, high fluorescence stability and religious biocompatibility, which can be used as a di-functional and label-free chem-nanosensor for the determination of pH and curcumin (Cur). When the pH is decreased from 8.0 to 4.4, the N,S-CNDs reveals an extraordinary emission escalation at emission wavelength (λem) of 543 nm, and the pK
a value of the N,S-CNDs was calculated as 6.06. The N,S-CNDs displays a favourable linear relationship in the physiological pH range of 5.0–7.4, which is meaningful for the research of near-neutral cytosolic pH. Furthermore, the laser scanning confocal microscopic images of intracellular distribution and the determination of pH in HeLa cells were successfully carried out, implying that the N,S-CNDs possess excellent cell membrane permeability and are capable of being further applied to real-time pH fluctuations monitoring in live cells with negligible autofluorescence. More importantly, the fluorescence of N,S-CNDs may be dramatically quenched by Cur via a combination of electrostatic and hydrogen-bond interaction and fluorescence inner filter effect (FIFE). The limit of detection is as low as 81 nmol/L Cur, and the linearity range is 0.5–20 μmol/L Cur. Ultimately, a satisfactory result was obtained when applying the as-constructed fluorescent chem-nanosensing system for the analyses of Cur in practical samples.

The as-fabricated nitrogen and sulfur dual-doped carbon nanodots (N,S-CNDs) could serve as a di-functional and label-free chem-nanosensor for real-time monitoring of pH fluctuation in live cells and quantitative determining of Curcumin in real samples.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Intracellular pH (pHi) [1] plays pivotal modulating roles in cell, enzyme and tissue functions, such as cell cycle control [2], cellular proliferation and apoptosis [3], ion transport [4], calcium regulation [5,6], muscle contraction [7] and multidrug resistance (MDR) [6]. The different pH homeostases exists in diverse prokaryotic species and diverse subcellular compartments of eukaryotic cells [8,9]. The cellular internalization pathways, including endocytosis [10], phagocytosis [11] and receptor-ligand internalization [12] are also controlled by the pH of cells. Abnormal pHi values can cause cardiopulmonary and neurologic problems such as cancer and Alzheimer's disease [13]. As such, the monitoring of pH changes in live cells is crucial in chemistry and biomedical fields. Many popular techniques, including microelectrodes, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and molecular spectroscopy have been used for the determination of pH in live cells. Among them, fluorescence spectroscopy has been a powerful tool for pH detection since it possesses some properties such as noninvasiveness, excellent sensitivity, fast response time, simple operation, high signal-to-noise ratio and the ability to continuously monitor the rapid kinetic pH changes [14] and therefore, developing a novel pHi determination method based on fluorescence spectroscopy is significative.

Curcumin (Cur), a polyphenolic pigment extracted and purified from the plant root of the Curcuma longa L., which attracts much interest because of its pharmacological and biological activities, including anti-inflammatory, anti-carcinogenic, anti-oxidant, anti-HIV, etc [15]. Nevertheless, recent researches have confirmed that the treatment of massive Cur displays pro-oxidant activity on DNA [16] as well as inducing intracellular ATP levels to decrease and trigger necrosis processes [17]. Consequently, Cur determination in human urine and food is of significant importance. The spectrofluorometry [18], UV–Vis spectrophotometry [19], capillary electrophoresis [20], voltammetric [21], HPLC [22], MALDI-TOF MS and UPLC-MS [23] methods have been developed to detect Cur; however, most of these methods are time-consuming and require expensive equipment or complicated sample pre-treatment procedure. The fluorimetry method took the fancy of the public because of its intrinsically high sensitivity, facile, rapid and cost-effective advantages. Therefore, the development of a rapid, sensitive and simple fluorescence method for Cur detection is an urgent need.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs), an up-rising star of the carbon-based nanomaterials, were firstly discovered at 2004 in the process of fabrication of single-walled carbon nanotubes using the arc-discharge method [24]. The CNDs have attracted much attention in recent years and have been reported to possess fantastic properties such as simple fabrication, low cost, good water solubility, high chemical stability, low toxicity, biological compatibility and environmental friendliness [25–28]. Due to these unique properties, the CNDs have been explored for applications in various fields, including bio-sensing and chem-probing [26], bioimaging [27], drug delivery [29], antibacterial aspects [30], batteries [31], thermal theranostics [32], photocatalysis [33] and electrocatalysis [34]. Currently, many efforts have been contributed to the fabrication of bi-functional and multi-functional fluorescent nanosensor based on CNDs for the determination of various species such as Cr(VI) [35], Mn(VII) [36], Fe3+ [37], temperature [38], AA [35,36], Au3+ [39], S2− [39], lifetime thermal [40], Zn2+ [41], Mn2+ [41], Cu2+ [41], etc. These nanosensors are capable of detecting multi-substances or perform multi-functions at the same time, further widening the application range of CNDs. Herein, we developed a di-functional and label-free fluorescent chem-nanosensor for simultaneous determination of pH and Cur, which is meaningful for the detection of live cells and real samples.

In this work, using acid fuchsin as carbon source and dopant, the nitrogen and sulfur dual-doped carbon nanodots (N,S-CNDs) with yellow fluorescence was fabricated by a one-pot hydrothermal treatment method. The as-prepared N,S-CNDs was triumphantly used as fluorescent chem-nanosensor for the detection of pHi. The biocompatibility and potential applications of the N,S-CNDs were explored. Besides, the laser scanning confocal microscopic (LSCM) images of the cellular distribution of the N,S-CNDs and the pH detection in live human cervical cancer (HeLa) cells were successfully performed, demonstrating that the N,S-CNDs have good cell membrane permeability and could image near-neutral cytosolic pH fluctuations in live cells. More importantly, we constructed a “turn off” fluorescent chem-nanosensor for Cur determination based on a combination of electrostatic and hydrogen-bond interaction and FIFE between N,S-CNDs and Cur. Finally, the N,S-CNDs-based fluorescent chem-nanosensor was used for the quantitative determination of Cur in real samples with satisfactory results.

Acid fuchsin (C20H17N3Na2O9S3), ethanol (C2H5OH), potassium chloride (KCl), different metal ion chloride, phosphoric acid (H3PO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), dibasic sodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), all the amino acids and twelve kinds of drugs (FA, MH, VC, TC, DH, GN, AD, OF, DY, HAS, DOX and Cur) were purchased from Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). All chemical reagents were of analytical grade or above and used directly without further purification. The preparation methods of the corresponding biological reagents and the double-distilled water (DDW) were referred to the published papers [29,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fabrication of nitrogen and sulfur dual-doped carbon nanodots (N,S-CNDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 0.1 g of acid fuchsin and 10 mL of ethanol were thoroughly mixed and transferred into a 50 mL autoclave, which was then heated at 250 °C for 12 h in an oven. The obtained ethanol solution in autoclave was orderly treated by evaporating ethanol, dissolving in DDW, centrifuging, dialyzing and lyophilizing, and finally the N,S-CNDs powder was obtained.

All analytical techniques including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), zeta potential, elemental analysis, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime assays were used to characterize the properties of N,S-CNDs. The apparatus and specific parameters were in consistent with our previously published papers [29,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of pH and fluorescence pH titrations
ce:section.ce:para: For pH fluorescence assay of N,S-CNDs, 100 μL of N,S-CNDs solution (2.0 mg/mL) was mixed with 1.9 mL of 10 mmol/L PBS buffers with various pHs. In the pH titration experiments, 3 mL of N,S-CNDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) was poured into a quartz optical cell with an optical path length of 1 cm, and slight pH variations of the solution were achieved by adding the minimum volumes of HCl (1.0 mmol/L, 10 mmol/L, 100 mmol/L, 500 mmol/L and 1.0 mol/L). The PL spectra were recorded after each addition with λex at 472 nm.

The human cervical cancer (HeLa) cells were selected to measure the cellular toxicity of the N,S-CNDs. HeLa cells were added into a 96-well cell culture cluster and cultured with 5% CO2 in air at 37 °C for 12 h to adhere cells onto the surface. Then, the medium were replaced by 200 μL fresh DMEM with 10% FBS containing different concentrations of N,S-CNDs. HeLa cells were allowed to grow for another 24 h. In the dark environment, 10 μL of CCK-8 mixed with 90 μL DMEM was added into every well and the cells were incubated for 30 min. At last, the optical density of the mixture in each well was measured at 450 nm using SunRise microplate reader (Tecan Austria GmbH, Grödig, Austria). The computational formula was proceeded according to our previously published work [29,37].

At 37 °C, the HeLa cells were seeded in the culture dish and incubated with 0.50 mg/mL N,S-CNDs at free DMEM culture medium for 1 h. Subsequently, HeLa cells were harvested and washed three times by pH 7.4 dulbecco's phosphate buffered saline (DPBS), and then treated with nigericin (2 mg/mL) in 1.0 mL DPBS (pH 7.4) for 20 min. Furthermore, the HeLa cells were rinsed three times again and incubated with 10 mmol/L PBS buffer at different pH (7.4, 6.0, and 5.0) for an additional 20 min. Ultimately, the HeLa cells images and the fluorescence intensity were monitored and recorded on a LSM-880 airy scan super-solution confocal microscope (Zeiss, German).

The concentration of 0.1 mg/mL N,S-CNDs were obtained by dissolving the freeze-dried N,S-CNDs powder in DDW and PBS (pH 6.0, 7.4 and 8.0), respectively. The aqueous solutions containing eighteen kinds of metal ions (K+, Na+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+), twenty-two kinds of amino acids (Trp, Tyr, Val, Phe, Leu, Gly, Asp, Met, Ala, Cys, Glu, Pro, Ser, Thr, Ile, His, Lys, Gln, Arg, Asn, Hcy and GSH) and twelve kinds of drugs (FA, MH, VC, TC, GH, Asp, AD, OF, Cam, BSA, DOX and Cur) were prepared at a concentration of 0.1 mol/L. To evaluate the selectivity of the N,S-CNDs chem-nanosensor, the PL intensities were measured by adding each 10 μL of the solutions of above-mentioned ions to 2.0 mL N,S-CNDs aqueous solution. The λex and λem were 472 and 543 nm, respectively. The fluorescence titration experiments of N,S-CNDs in DDW and PBS by Cur were used to calculate the linear equation.

Ginger juice, Curry powder, Mustard, Ground ginger and Radix curcuma were respectively bought from Walmart supermarket and medicamentarius in Taiyuan, Shanxi, China and dried for 7 days at room temperature to remove the moisture before use. Ginger juice and Mustard were frozen, lyophilized, and then kept in a desiccator. Prior to extraction, each item was ground to a fine powder using a grinder. The pulverized samples were respectively dissolved in ethanol by ultrasonic to prepare the solutions at a concentration of 1.0 g/mL. These above-mentioned solutions were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min in a refrigerated centrifuge and were evaporated to obtain solid powder. The powder was re-dissolved in ethanol to form clear supernatant (1.0 g/mL). All the supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μm pore size filter membrane and then diluted with DDW to an appropriate concentration in the linear detection range.

The Cur content determined by an Agilent 1260 series (Boeblingen, Germany) HPLC consisting of a quaternary pump, an Agilent 1260 series variable wavelength detector (VWD), a ChemStation software and an Agilent C18 column (4.6 mm × 250 mm; 5 μm, Agilent Technologies Inc., California, USA). The mobile phase was composed of acetonitrile and 5% acetic acid in DDW (60:40, v/v) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min with the column temperature of 25 °C. The mobile phase was filtered through a 0.45 μm nylon membrane filter and ultrasonically degassed prior to use. The chromatograms were obtained by monitoring the UV detector at 420 nm. The volume of injection was 250 μL and the analysis time was 5 min per sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of samples and calibration curves
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained solid powder in 2.8 was dissolved in ethanol to form 0.05 g/mL solution and then filtered through 0.45 μm syringe filter prior to HPLC analysis. Cur standard (1 mg/mL) was made with ethanol. Working standard solutions for calibration curves were prepared by diluting the stock solution with ethanol at seven different concentration levels (1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40 and 50 μg/mL). All the stock and working solutions were kept at −18 °C in a freezer.

The relative FQY of the N,S-CNDs was calculated according to equation (1):
(1)
Φ
x = Φ
st (I
x /I
st )(A
st /A
x )(η
x
2/η
st
2)
where I, A and η are the fluorescence intensities, optical densities (OD), and the refractive index, respectively. The subscript "x" and "st" refer to the sample and the standard, respectively. For these aqueous solutions, η
x /η
st = 1. Fluorescein in 0.1 mol/L NaOH, which possesses a FQY of 0.79 at λex = 495 nm, was used as a standard. The OD and fluorescence intensities were measured on a Lambda 950 spectrophotometer and a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrofluorometer, respectively.

The synthetic route of the N,S-CNDs is depicted in Scheme 1 . The N,S-CNDs were obtained by hydrothermal treatment of acid fuchsin at 250 °C for 12 h. In this system, acid fuchsin served as carbon source and dopant, providing sufficient carbon, nitrogen and sulfur and facilitating the formation of nitrogen and sulfur dual-doped carbon nanodots. Elemental analysis was initially used to characterize the doping of heteroatoms into N,S-CNDs. The C, H, N, S and O are found in the as-fabricated N,S-CNDs (Table S1A), and their corresponding contents are 9.02, 2.52, 3.35, 18.25 and 66.86 wt%, respectively. Moreover, the elemental contents of the N,S-CNDs expressed by the relative number of each atom were displayed in Table S1B. The empirical formula of a N,S-CND is approximately C6H21N2S5O35, indicating that N and S from acid fuchsin are successfully doped into the N,S-CNDs.

FTIR measurement was used to identify the doping of N and S into N,S-CNDs and to investigate the differences in functionalities between N,S-CNDs and acid fuchsin. Fig. 1 A shows the FTIR spectra of acid fuchsin (blank line) and the N,S-CNDs (red line). In the FTIR spectrum of the N,S-CNDs, a broad absorption peak (∼3399-3561 cm−1) and two sharp absorption peaks (2991 cm−1 and 1023 cm−1) are found, which are attributed to the O-H/N-H stretching, C-H and C-O-C stretching, respectively [37,42–44]. These functional groups are also observed in the FTIR spectrum of acid fuchsin (blank line in Fig. 1A), indicating that these functional groups were derived from acid fuchsin. In addition, there are some characteristic absorption peaks corresponding to C=N (1636 cm−1), amido CON-H (1509 cm−1) and C-S (1145 cm−1) stretching in the FTIR spectrum of N,S-CNDs [37,43,44], demonstrating that carboxylic amido and alkyl sulfide functionalities exist in N,S-CNDs. The intensive C-S stretching (1145 cm−1) band in N,S-CNDs suggests that the initial S-O (1193 cm−1) in acid fuchsin has been decomposed and S was incorporated into N,S-CNDs. In general, at the high-temperature hydrothermal treatment progress, acid fuchsin was thermal decomposed and the N and S heteroatoms were doped into the N,S-CNDs, resulting in the formation of the N,S-CNDs functionalized with carboxylic acid, amido and alkyl sulfide moieties. Subsequently, to further gain insights into the element states and surface functional groups of the N,S-CNDs, XPS analyses were performed. As can be seen from Fig. 1B, the XPS survey scan of the N,S-CNDs reveals five peaks at 166.1, 229.8, 282.3, 397.9 and 528.9 eV, corresponding to S 2p, S 2s, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 1C) can be resolved into four peaks with binding energy at 286.7, 285.5, 284.9 and 284.5 eV, which are corresponding to C-O, C-N/C=N, C-S-C and C=C bonds, respectively [37,43]. Fig. 1D exhibits the N 1s spectrum which can be de-convoluted into two peaks at 401.9 and 399.5 eV, indicating N 1s states in N-(C)3/pyrrolic N and C-N-C, respectively [44]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) is fitted with four peaks at 532.8, 532.2, 531.9 and 531.6 eV, which are assigned to O=C-O∗, C-OH/C-O-C, ∗O=C-O and C-N-O, respectively [29,37,44]. As shown in Fig. 1F, the high-resolved S 2p XPS could be fitted into four peaks at 170.0, 169.2, 169.0 and 168.6 eV, which are attributed to S2p 3/2 C-S, S2p C-S-N, S2p 3/2 C-S-O and S2p 1/2 C-S, respectively [43,44]. The full width at half maximum (FWHM), peak area (S) and percentage (%) of all the peaks de-convoluted from C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S 2p were exhibited in Table S2, and the results are in consistent with the C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S 2p XPS spectrum, respectively. All XPS data exhibit that the surface of the N,S-CNDs is functionalized with C-N, C-S-C, C-S-O, C-N-O, C=O, C-O, etc. These functional groups endow N,S-CNDs with excellent water solubility, which benefits the construction of fluorescence sensors (vide infra). In summary, the results of elemental analysis, FTIR and XPS demonstrated that the surface of the N,S-CNDs is dual-doped with N and S heteroatoms.

As depicted in Fig. S1, the zeta potential (ζ) of N,S-CNDs is 10.50 mV, which demonstrates that the abundant functional groups impart the N,S-CNDs with positive charges. The thermo-stability of acid fuchsin and N,S-CNDs were investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in anitrogen (N2) atmosphere (Fig. 2 ). For acid fuchsin, the loss of weight are about 9.8%, 16.1%, 31.1% and 29.9% at 300 °C, 500 °C, 760 °C and 950 °C, respectively. The residual weight is only 14.1%. Nevertheless, N,S-CNDs exhibit good thermal stability up to 200 °C with loss of weight of only 15% corresponding to the loss of adsorbed water. The loss of weight at 200–600 °C is about 16%, inferring the entire carbonization and pyrolysis of N,S-CNDs with the releasing of pyrogens (CO, CO2, NO, NO2and SO2). At 600−950 °C, the TGA curve becomes horizontal, indicating that nitrogen, sulfur and the incomplete carbonized fraction of N,S-CNDs transforms to nitrogen, sulfur, oxygen and/or carbon materials with a yield of almost 69%. These results illustrate that the acid fuchsin has been wholly carbonized to N,S-CNDs by hydrothermal method, and the obtained N,S-CNDs possess favourable thermo-stability in comparison with acid fuchsin and other CNDs [29].

Subsequently, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed to investigate the morphologies of the as-prepared N,S-CNDs. The representative TEM image of the N,S-CNDs is displayed in Fig. 3 A. It can be seen that most of N,S-CNDs particles have spherical morphology and are dispersed rather evenly on the TEM grid surface. The corresponding histogram is depicted in Fig. 3B, which obtained by statistical analysis of approximately 100 particles using the Nano Measurer software. The Gaussian fitting curve proves that the as-fabricated N,S-CNDs has a corresponding particle size distribution in the range of 2.0–4.1 nm with an average diameter of 2.99 ± 0.2 nm. Moreover, a magnified TEM image of the N,S-CNDs (the upper-right corner of Fig. 3A) shows that the lattice spacing is ca. 0.20 nm, which is consistent with the previously reported lattice spacing of CNDs [37,45,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the as-fabricated N,S-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were acquired for exploring the optical properties of the as-fabricated N,S-CNDs, which are displayed in Fig. 4 . As depicted by Fig. 4A (blank line), a cuspidal absorption peak at ca. 240 nm and an acromion at ca. 266 nm are observed, which are attributed to the π→π * transition of the aromatic sp2 domain and the n→π * transition of C=O [29,42–45], respectively. In addition, a prominent absorption peak at ca. 315 nm is found, probably ascribing to the formation of excited defect surface states induced by the N and S heteroatoms [29,42,43]. The optimal excitation (λex) and emission (λem) peaks of N,S-CNDs are located at 472 and 543 nm, respectively (Fig. 4A). The photograph of N,S-CNDs dispersion under a UV lamp (λex = 365 nm) exhibits strong yellow emission (inset of Fig. 4A). The fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CNDs under various λex were investigated and are displayed in Fig. 4B. The λem peaks of N,S-CNDs remain constant at ca. 440 nm and 532–545 nm when the λex are 320–340 and 360–480 nm, respectively; nevertheless, it is red-shifted from 545 to 585 nm when the λex moves from 500 to 540 nm. The λex-independent and λex-dependent fluorescence spectra are probably corresponding to the π *→π transitions of the graphitic structure of the carbon cores and the π *→n transitions of the surface-attached functionalities (C=C/C-O/C-N/C-S-C/C-N-O/C-S-O), respectively. The λex-dependent fluorescence behavior has been commonly seen in previously reported CNDs [29,37,42–45]. The λex-dependent fluorescence spectra are gradually shifted with the variation of λex, illustrating that the λem could be tuned by controlling λex of the N,S-CNDs (vide infra). The FQY of the as-prepared N,S-CNDs was measured to be 9.9% by using fluorescein as a reference.

The fluorescence stability of the N,S-CNDs under various ionic strengths, metal cation ions, and Xe lamp irradiation were explored. As depicted by Fig. S2A, when the N,S-CNDs was impacted with the ultra-high concentration (2.0 mol/L) of KCl, the spectral feature and fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CNDs hardly change, illustrating that the N,S-CNDs is suitable for applications in a physiological environment. Fig. S2B displays the effect of different kinds of metal cation ions on the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CNDs. There are no obvious changes (94–106%) in fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CNDs in the presence of various metal ions at ultra-high concentration of approximately 10 mmol/L. As depicted by Fig. S2C, only 28% fluorescence quenching rate is observed after sustaining Xe lamp irradiation for 3 h, indicating that the fluorescence of the N,S-CNDs is stable. For further demonstrating the stability of the fabricated N,S-CNDs, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the N,S-CNDs in different concentration levels of KCl and various metal ions were obtained, and is depicted in Fig. S3. The absorption spectra of the N,S-CNDs kept unchangeable under high concentration KCl (Fig. S3A) and metal ions (Fig. S3B), illustrating that the N,S-CNDs possess stable optical performance. The obtained N,S-CNDs powder could be dispersed repeatedly in DDW without any aggregation, indicating that the N,S-CNDs is beneficial to preserve and transport. After the prepared N,S-CNDs solution was stored in ambient conditions for six months, it still exhibits a homogeneous phase without any precipitation, demonstrating the N,S-CNDs possess long-term colloidal stability. These prominent properties of the N,S-CNDs offers potential applications (vide infra).

The determination of pH is critical importance, attributing to pH plays a key role in many biological and chemical systems. The effectof pH on absorption and the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CNDs is investigated and the results are illustrated in Fig. S4. As shown by Fig. S4A, the strong acid and alkali do not affect the absorption of the N,S-CNDs, indicating that the N,S-CNDs could tolerate the extreme conditions. The fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CNDs increases as pH decreases from 8.0 to 2.0 and reaches the highest at pH 4.5–2.0; however, the fluorescence intensity maintains fairly constant when the pH is higher than 8.0 (Fig. S4B). Since the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CNDs is significantly changed at the weakly-acid and physiological pH 4.4–8.0, the N,S-CNDs could be potentially applied as pH chem-nanosensor. As shown by Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CNDs is changed within a broad range of pH 4.4–8.0 under λex of 472 nm. The N,S-CNDs possess optically pH-sensitive property; the fluorescence intensity increases with the decreases in pH 8.0–4.4, demonstrating that the N,S-CNDs could serve as an attractive fluorescent chem-nanosensor for pH-monitoring. Fig. 5B depicts the plot of fluorescence intensity against pH at 8.0–4.4, which was fitted by sigmoidal curve using the Boltzmann equation. Owing to the pH fluctuation within narrow limits under the normal physiological conditions, the fluorescence intensity changes of the N,S-CNDs in the range of nearly neutral pH is investigated. It can be seen that, a great linear relationship between pH and fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CNDs is observed at pH 5.0 to 7.4, and the linear equation (2) is described as follows:
(2) (F
0 -F)/F
0 = 0.38 pH–1.89 (R
2 = 0.995)
where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the N,S-CNDs at pH 5.0 and other pHs, respectively (Fig. 5C). The pK
a values of 6.06 and the slope of 6.20 were obtained by fitting the pH and fluorescence intensity with the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation. With this information, the N,S-CNDs can be used to establish a “Mostly-Off → Partially-On → Entirely-On” nanosensor at pH 7.4, 6.0 and 5.0, respectively, which could be used for real-time monitoring the pH fluctuation in living cells (vide infra).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and application of the N,S-CNDs in real-time pH-monitoring of live cells
ce:section.ce:para: Based on these encouraging results and the excellent characteristics of the N,S-CNDs, we further assessed the probability of the N,S-CNDs as a new fluorescent chem-nanosensor for living cellular imaging and real-time pH-monitoring in live cells. One important factor for effective cellular imaging is that the selected fluorescent chem-nanosensor possesses outstanding optical properties and ultra-low cytotoxicity. To evaluate the cytotoxicity of the N,S-CNDs, the viability of HeLa cells treated with the N,S-CNDs was tested using the classical MTT method. The HeLa cells were incubated in the culture medium with the concentration of the N,S-CNDs at 0, 50, 100, 200, 400, and 800 μg/mL for 24 h, respectively. As depicted by Fig. S5, the cell inhibition rate gradually enhances with the increase in the concentration of the N,S-CNDs; fortunately, the increases in the inhibition rates are not too much. The inhibition rates are less than 16% even in the presence of an ultra-high concentration (800 μg/mL) of the N,S-CNDs. These results show that the as-fabricated N,S-CNDs display low toxicity to the HeLa cells, inferring its potential to be used for imaging of live cells or other biomedical applications.

To further explore the potential application of the N,S-CNDs for living cellular imaging, the HeLa cells were exposed to the N,S-CNDs solutions and then were incubated with nigericin, which can equilibrate the intracellular and extracellular pH in three different PBS buffers (pH = 7.4, 6.0 and 5.0) [47,48]. As depicted by Fig. 6 , at pH 7.4, the HeLa cells stained with the N,S-CNDs exhibit weakest yellow emissions (Fig. 6D and G). When the pHs drop to 6.0 and 5.0, the HeLa cells emit yellow emissions of the moderate intensity (Fig. 6E and H) and the strongest intensity (Fig. 6F and I), respectively. However, the yellow emissions of the HeLa cells are quenched when the pH switches back to 7.4, indicating the emission reversibility of the N,S-CNDs in the HeLa cells. Furthermore, the N,S-CNDs could pass through cell membranes to reach the cytoplasm but rarely penetrate into the cell nuclei, which illustrating the N,S-CNDs could avoid the gene disruption. The intracellular fluorescence intensities (λex/λem at 488/490–600 nm) of the N,S-CNDs at pH 7.4, 6.0 and 5.0 are depict in Fig. 6J, respectively, demonstrating that the HeLa cells emit the fluorescence intensities at pH 7.4 < pH 6.0 < pH 5.0. Fig. S6 illustrates the λex-dependent fluorescence emission of the N,S-CNDs-stained cells at pH 7.4, 6.0 and 5.0, respectively. The cells exhibit yellow and red emissions at λex of 488 and 543 nm, respectively. Again, the fluorescence intensities of the HeLa cells follow an order of pH 7.4 < pH 6.0 < pH 5.0. These results further demonstrate that the as-constructed “Mostly-Off → Partially-On → Entirely-On” fluorescent chem-nanosensor based on the N,S-CNDs are efficient fluorescent pH chem-nanosensor for reversibly real-time monitoring of the pH fluctuation in live cells. In particular, in acidulated pH environment, the “Turn-On” fluorescent chem-nanosensor is more sensitive than the “Turn-Off” fluorescence sensor [44], demonstrating that our as-constructed “Mostly-Off → Partially-On → Entirely-On” fluorescent chem-nanosensor possesses higher application values in bioimaging and biomedical applications.

The effects of various amino acids and drugs on the absorption and fluorescence spectra of N,S-CNDs were studied. It is observed that these amino acids and drugs do not affect the absorption spectra (Fig. S7) of the N,S-CNDs. Some substances (Trp, MH, TC, Cam and Cur) have respective absorption peaks and as such the corresponding absorption spectra possess the characteristic absorption peaks of the N,S-CNDs and that of these substances, manifesting that the UV absorption properties of the N,S-CNDs are stable under various amino acids and drugs. Each amino acids, including Trp, Tyr, Val, Phe, Leu, Gly, Asp, Met, Ala, Cys, Glu, Pro, Ser, Thr, Ile, His, Lys, Gln, Arg, Asn, Hcy and GSH and various of drugs, including FA, MH, VC, TC, GH, Asp, AD, OF, Cam, BSA, DOX and Cur at a concentration of 10 mmol/L were reacted with the N,S-CNDs solution (0.1 mg/mL), respectively. No obvious changes (90–101%) in the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CNDs are observed in the presence of different kinds of amino acids (Fig. S8A), illustrating that the N,S-CNDs possess stabilized fluorescence property in the presence of these amino acids. These results are beneficial to the practical applications of the N,S-CNDs in the fields of cellular and animal imaging (vide supra). As shown by Fig. S8B, among the tested drugs, Cur displays the strongest fluorescence quenching effect on the N,S-CNDs. Based on this phenomenon, we constructed a “turn-off” fluorescence chem-nanosensor for the determination of Cur. Since the N,S-CNDs possess pH-dependent PL behavior, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CNDs/Cur was investigated. As depicted by Fig. S9, the quenching efficiency in DDW is higher than that at the near neutral pH range of 4.0–10. Fig. 7 A describes the changes in the fluorescence of N,S-CNDs in the presence of different contents of Cur. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CNDs solution are gradually depressed as the Cur concentration increases from 0.0 to 80 μmol/L. Figs. 7B and S10 exhibit the Stern-Volmer (SV) plot of the changes of fluorescent intensities of N,S-CNDs in DDW and at different pHs (6.0, 7.4 and 8.0) with the increase of Cur concentration (F
0
/F against Cur concentration). The fluorescence quenching efficiency of N,S-CNDs is fitted by SV equation (3):
(3)
F
0
/F = 1 + K
SV [Q]
where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N,S-CNDs without and with Cur at λex/λem of 472/543 nm, respectively. And K
SV and [Q] are the SV quenching constant and the concentration of Cur, respectively. When the N,S-CNDs was dissolved in DDW, pH 6.0, pH 7.4 and pH 8.0, the K
SV are calculated to be 2.1 × 104, 3.8 × 103, 3.0 × 103 and 6.4 × 103 L/mol with a correlation coefficient R
2 of 0.9918, 0.9929, 0.9905 and 0.99, respectively. As shown by Table S3, when the N,S-CNDs was dissolved in DDW, pH 6.0, pH 7.4 and pH 8.0, the F
0
/F curve is linearly dependent on the Cur concentration in the range of 0.5–20, 1.5–36, 2.2–136 and 1.9–41 μmol/L, respectively, demonstrating the excellent sensing property of the N,S-CNDs towards trace Cur. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated by 3 times and 10 times the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity at the blank (n = 12) divided by K
SV , respectively [37,42]. The LOD and LOQ of the as-constructed chem-nanosensor at DDW, pH 6.0, pH 7.4 and pH 8.0 were calculated to be 81 (29.84 ng/mL) and 267 nmol/L (98.36 ng/mL), 451.2 (166.2 ng/mL) and 1504 nmol/L (554.1 ng/mL), 576.6 (212.4 ng/mL) and 1922 nmol/L (708.1 ng/mL), and 267.9 (98.7 ng/mL) and 892.9 nmol/L (328.9 ng/mL), respectively, indicating that the N,S-CNDs with sensitive response could be employed as a chem-nanosensor for the quantification of Cur. Considering the sensitivity of the chem-nanosensor and the stability of Cur, N,S-CNDs in DDW was chosen for all the subsequent detection assays. Furthermore, a comparison of our work and the previously proposed CNDs-based analytical methods [49,51] employed for Cur determination is listed in Table 1 .

Cur is an important anti-cancer and anti-inflammatory drug, the proposed fluorescent chem-nanosensor based on N,S-CNDs is conductive to determine the concentration of Cur in urine sample, food and drug. The recovery was detected by standard addition of different known concentrations of Cur and the results of the above determinations were listed in Table 2 . As shown in Table 2A, the Cur contents in Human urine, Ginger juice, Curry powder, Mustard, Ground ginger and Radix curcuma detected by the constructed chem-nanosensor are 0.00 μmol/L (0.00 μg/mL), 5.53 μmol/L (2.04 μg/mL), 3.57 μmol/L (1.32 μg/mL), 3.05 μmol/L (1.12 μg/mL), 4.39 μmol/L (1.62 μg/mL) and 6.64 μmol/L (2.45 μg/mL), respectively, with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) in the range of 4.18–5.60% (n = 6). The quantitative spike recoveries for the determination of Cur in these above-mentioned real samples range from 96.6 to 104.6% with the RSDs ˂ 5.56% (n = 6, Table 2B). Furthermore, in order to validate the accuracy of the proposed method, a high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was used to measure the Cur in real samples. The linear equation between the concentration of Cur and integral area was constructed (Fig. S11). As shown by Table S4, the contents of Cur in Human urine, Ginger juice, Curry powder, Mustard, Ground ginger and Radix curcuma are calculated as 0.00, 2.29, 1.25, 1.10, 1.67 and 2.76 μg/mL, respectively. The corresponding chromatograms of Human urine, Ginger juice, Curry powder, Mustard, Ground ginger and Radix curcuma are depicted in Fig. S12. These results demonstrated that there is a good agreement between the results obtained by our proposed fluorescent chem-nanosensor and by HPLC, indicating that the N,S-CNDs is capable of label-free and rapid monitoring of the amount of Cur in real samples with accurate results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the recognition of curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the fluorescence mechanism excited by UV lamp is due to the existence of conjugated system in Cur. As shown in Fig. S13A, the λex and λem peaks of Cur are located at 412 and 559 nm, respectively. When the λex is at 472 nm, the fluorescence intensity of Cur is decreased to 60% of the maximum fluorescence intensity. Fig. 7A depicts the fluorescence spectra of the as-synthesized N,S-CNDs in the presence of different concentrations of Cur. Apparently, the maximum fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CNDs at the λem of 543 nm decreases with the increase of the concentration of Cur. No difference is observed in the emission center of the N,S-CNDs in the absence and in the presence of Cur. One the potential mechanism, that is as static quenching, for the recognition of Cur by N,S-CNDs is depicted in Fig. 7C. As shown in Fig. S13B, the characteristic absorption peak is located at 421 nm. As indicated by Fig. S13C, the absorption intensity of the N,S-CNDs at the wavelength of 421 nm increases with the increase of the concentration of Cur, whereas the maximum fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CNDs at the λem of 543 nm decreases with the increase of the concentration of Cur (Fig. 7A). These results demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CNDs is probably due to the strong interactions between N,S-CNDs and Cur. One of the interactions may result from the formation of the hydrogen bonds between N,S-CNDs and Cur. The NH/C-S-N/CON-H groups are present on the surface of N,S-CNDs (Fig. 1), and the OH/R-O-CH3/C=O groups are present in the structure of Cur (Fig. 7C). The NH/C-S-N/CON-H/OH groups could act as either hydrogen bond donors or acceptors while R-O-CH3/C=O groups could act as hydrogen bond acceptors. Thus, the hydrogen bonds between N,S-CNDs and Cur can readily form to shorten the spatial distance. On the other hand, after adding Cur to the N,S-CNDs, the color of N,S-CNDs changes from colorless to dark yellow (inset in Fig. S13C), which results in the depressed transmittance and the fluorescence self-quenching of the N,S-CNDs. Another potential mechanism for the recognition of Cur by N,S-CNDs is the fluorescence inner filter effect (FIFE) (Fig. 7D). Approximately 60–70% excitation light was shielded by Cur, which weakens the energy of the excitation wavelength, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CNDs. It is possible that the other analytes could have static interaction with N,S-CNDs; however, the reduced light transmittance and FIFE could only be happen between Cur and N,S-CNDs.

Another fundamental attempt usually used to study the static quenching and FIFE between molecules is to examine the change of fluorescence lifetime of the donor/fluorophore [51]. The fluorescence lifetimes of the donor/fluorophore remain unchanged within the margin of error when electrostatic and hydrogen-bonding interaction and FIFE take place. The fluorescence lifetimes of the generated N,S-CNDs were determined in the absence and in the presence of different concentrations of Cur, and the results are displayed in Fig. 7E and Table S5. In the absence of Cur (the black curve in Fig. 7E), the average lifetime of N,S-CNDs is 4.83 ns (χ
2 = 1.074) with lifetimes of τ
1 = 2.40 ns (53.48%) and τ
2 = 7.62 ns (46.52%). In the presence of different concentration of Cur, the average fluorescence lifetimes of the N,S-CNDs are between 4.71 ns and 4.84 ns (Table S5). The nearly no change in the lifetime of the N,S-CNDs indicate that there is no significant FRET between N,S-CNDs and Cur, demonstrating that the fluorescence change of the N,S-CNDs is resulted from the electrostatic and hydrogen-bonding interaction and FIFE between N,S-CNDs and Cur and the fluorescence self-quenching effect from the change of the color. More importantly, N,S-CNDs, Cur and the mixture of N,S-CNDs and Cur (1.0 μmol/L) are positively charged (Zeta potential: +10.50 mV, Fig. S14), negatively charged (Zeta potential: -5.75 mV, Fig. S14) and near-neutrally charged (Zeta potential: +3.18 mV, Fig. S14), respectively, indicating that the intense electrostatic and hydrogen-bonding interaction took place between N,S-CNDs and Cur. The result facilitates the demonstration of the present concept that the fluorescence chem-nanosensing is clearly based on the static quenching and FIFE rather than the other possible approaches.

Herein, we constructed a rapid and label-free fluorescent chem-nanosensor for sensitive and selective determination of pH and Cur based on N,S-CNDs. The N,S-CNDs were fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal reaction using acid fuchsin as sole carbon, nitrogen and sulfur source. The as-prepared N,S-CNDs exhibit yellow fluorescent, high anti-photobleaching, low cytotoxicity and the respectable FQY is up to 9.9%, which was confirmed as a di-functional and label-free chem-nanosensor for pH and Cur detection. N,S-CNDs displays pH-dependent fluorescence behavior; the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CNDs is very strong at pH 4.4 but is very weak at pH 8.0. In addition, N,S-CNDs depict a linear response to the physiological pH in the range of 5.0–7.4, which can be applied as a “Turn-On" fluorescent chem-nanosensor to noninvasively real-time monitor pH fluctuations in living cells. In particular, under the physiological conditions used for the disease diagnosis, chem-nanosensing and biomedical fields, N,S-CNDs can be as a promising candidate for real-time tracking and monitoring of the intracellular pH fluctuations. More importantly, the fluorescent intensity of the N,S-CNDs is sensitive towards Cur detection in a wide linear range of 0.5–20 μmol/L with a LOD of 81 nmol/L. Compared to the previously reported CNDs-based sensors/probes, the as-constructed N,S-CNDs-based chem-nanosensor provides better selectivity and higher sensitively and can be successfully applied for Cur determination in real samples with satisfactory results.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705101 and 21575084) and the Postgraduate Student Education Innovation Project (No.2016BY005). We acknowledge Dr. Juanjuan Wang from the Scientific Instrument Center at Shanxi University for her help with LSCM measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2019.01.012.

Cu-N dopants boost electron transfer and photooxidation reactions of carbon dots

On-off-on fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect

Effective determination of Zn2+, Mn2+, and Cu2+ simultaneouslyby using dual-emissive carbon dots as colorimetric fluorescentprobe
An "on-off-on"fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot
Green synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen/sulfur-doped carbon dots and investigation of their properties by HPLC coupled with mass spectrometry
Low temperature synthesis of phosphorous and nitrogen co-doped yellow fluorescent carbon dots for sensing and bioimaging
Highly fluorescent N, S-co-doped carbon dots and their potential applications as antioxidants and sensitive probes for Cr (VI) detection
Ethanol in aqueous hydrogen peroxide solution: hydrothermal synthesis of highlyphotoluminescentcarbon dots as multifunctionalnanosensors
A novel far-visible and near-infrared pH probe for monitoring near-neutral physiological pH changes: imaging in live cells
A ratiometric fluorescent pH probe based on aggregation-induced emission enhancement and its application in live-cell imaging

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent probefor detection of curcumin based on the inner filter effect
